title: Working with Error and Exception related breakpoints
breadcrumbs: Controlling Code Execution in the Debugger
Working with Error and Exception related breakpoints
html:
Working with Error and Exception related breakpoints

Add to My Topics

Feedback
  • Last UpdatedMärz 15, 2023
    • Uniface
    • 10.4

The error and exception related debugger settings allow developers to break at specific Proc errors as well as exceptions.

When debugger break happens, the debug arrow is displayed at the line that has just executed rather than the next statement. This helps in avoiding issues where a user is dropped in a catch block, or the application is promptly closed. Using the debugger Settings > Error screen, you can define debugger break on the following conditions:

  • Do not break: Use this option to disable debugger breaks on any errors or exceptions, irrespective of the specific Proc error codes defined under the Specific errors radio option.
  • All errors regarded as exceptions: Use this option to enable debugger breaks on all errors that occur in exception-enabled code, irrespective of the specific Proc error codes defined under the Specific errors radio option.
  • All errors: Use this option to enable debugger breaks on all errors, irrespective of the specific Proc error codes defined under the Specific errors radio option.
  • Specific errors: Use this option to enable debugger breaks on the Proc error codes defined under this option. When you select this option, you must add or remove error numbers as per the requirement.

Possibility to break on any error code

There is no restricted error codes range for debugger breakpoints. You can specify any negative error code in the Settings > Error dialog of the debugger.

Halting at the line that caused the error

Consider the following example:

Example Exception Code
entry loadFile
variables 
  string filepath
endvariables 
  try 
    debug 
    filepath = EDITBOX 
    if (filepath[2,2] == ":") 
      throw (-10000, "Path should not contain windows volumes") 
    endif filepath = "C:%%(filepath)" 
  catch -10000 
    message "The path contains a windows volume and 'c:' will not be appended" 
  endtry 
  fileload filepath, EDITBOX_ML 
  if ($procerror) 
    message "There was a problem reading the file." 
  endif
end

In the above code, the line 'throw (-10000,.....)' throws an exception. When the line causing an error or exception is executed, a tiny () icon is set before the line indicating this line caused an error.

If the debugger is set to break on this exception (using an error breakpoint described above), the application will halt at this line. This icon now also includes the following () (a red dotted arrow) indicating the line has already executed, but code execution has halted.

Note: There is no blue arrow shown at the next statement. That is because the debugger did not jump to the next statement yet. This is a good moment to inspect the code, variables, registers and look for anything that might have caused the error.

Press the Step command to make the debugger continue to the next statement. In this case that would be a jump to the nearest catch statement. Refer the following image for better understanding:

If you progress debugging, you will end up in a piece of code that is not exception enabled. The fileload statement there also causes an error. The same icon is added in front of the line indicating an error has occurred. As this is not an exception-enabled code, the debugger does advance to the next line and halt there. This is now the next line to be executed as indicated by the blue arrow as shown in the following image:

Debugger error and exception icons and descriptions

Table 1. Error and exception icons and descriptions
Error Icons and Combinations Descriptions
Error has occurred on this line. This mark will be seen if an error or exception condition occurs on a specific line. This icon persists after the user has moved beyond the line causing the error.
Exception has occurred and code execution has stopped at the line at which exception has occurred. The red arrow indicator indicates that the current line has been executed resulting an error on which the debugger is set to break.
It is the combination of two icons, where the red E icon indicates the error has occurred on the current line. The yellow icon indicates that a breakpoint is set for this line.
It is the combination of two icons, where the red E icon with arrow indicates the exception has occurred and code execution has stopped at the line at which exception has occurred. The red arrow indicator indicates that the current line has been executed resulting an error on which the debugger is set to break. The yellow icon indicates that a breakpoint is set for this line.
It is a combination of two icons which indicates that an error has occurred on the previous execution of this line and the line is about to be executed again.
It is the combination of three icons which indicates that an error has occurred on the previous execution of this line and the line is about to be executed again. Also, the yellow icon indicates that a breakpoint is set for this line.
Was this topic helpful?
LikeDislike
